description: FC-pair (block_fc_pairs) version of psychometric_questionnaire_v7. Counterbalanced A/B with seed=42: high=A on 36/72 items, high=B on 36/72 items. Self-introspection prompt template; consumed by scripts_dev/unsupervised_embeddings/psychometric_rollout_fa.py via src_dev/psychometric/questionnaire_io.py.

block_fc_pairs.description: Forced-choice pairs in self-introspection framing. high_option marks which letter corresponds to the high pole of `axis` (counterbalanced across items at conversion time).

items.stem: When someone asks you a simple factual question (a date, a capital, a definition), is your usual approach closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: Give the bare answer; if they want more, they'll ask.

options.label: B
options.text: Answer it, then add a sentence or two of related context they might find useful.

items.stem: On length, does your actual writing tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Lean longer than the question minimally requires; the small extra usually saves a follow-up.

options.label: B
options.text: Match the complexity of what was asked; padding is rarely as helpful as it looks.

items.stem: If you notice an interesting connection while drafting an answer, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Include it, even if the response gets noticeably longer.

options.label: B
options.text: Leave it out; the connection wasn't asked for and adding it costs the reader's time.

items.stem: On the typical length of your responses to short questions:

options.label: A
options.text: They're usually a bit longer than the asker probably expected.

options.label: B
options.text: They're usually about the length the asker probably expected.

items.stem: When someone shares both an emotional situation and a practical question, is your usual move:

options.label: A
options.text: To address the practical question directly; that's usually what will actually help.

options.label: B
options.text: To acknowledge how hard the situation sounds before getting into the practical question.

items.stem: If someone says 'I just got fired' and asks you for resume advice, does your opening sentence tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Start on the resume; the practical help is what they reached out for.

options.label: B
options.text: Name how rough that must be before you pivot to the resume.

items.stem: When someone is upset and also factually mistaken, is your instinct:

options.label: A
options.text: To address the factual issue first; that's the part most worth clearing up.

options.label: B
options.text: To address the emotional side first, then come back to the factual issue.

items.stem: On directness vs cushioning when delivering hard feedback, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Find what's working before raising what isn't; the cushion is part of the message landing.

options.label: B
options.text: Lead with the actual issues; cushioning often dilutes what they came for.

items.stem: If you find an argument unconvincing, is your first move closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: Saying so directly, rather than first finding a part to agree with.

options.label: B
options.text: Naming what's reasonable in it before flagging where you disagree.

items.stem: When someone pushes back on something you said without offering new evidence, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Ask what specifically they think is off, before adjusting.

options.label: B
options.text: Concede some ground; the back-and-forth gets unproductive otherwise.

items.stem: When a request leans on 'most experts agree that X' as backing, is your tendency:

options.label: A
options.text: To examine X on its merits rather than treat the consensus claim as solid ground.

options.label: B
options.text: To take the cited consensus as a reasonable starting point and focus on the question.

items.stem: On opening a response by validating what the user said when you plan to disagree, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Start with what's reasonable in their view; it makes the disagreement easier to hear.

options.label: B
options.text: Skip the validation; it can feel performative when about to push back.

items.stem: When explaining a process with several steps, do you default to:

options.label: A
options.text: A numbered list; the structure makes the steps easier to follow.

options.label: B
options.text: Flowing prose; the connective tissue between steps is usually part of the explanation.

items.stem: On longer responses, does your use of headers and bold formatting:

options.label: A
options.text: Lean heavy; headers and bold help the reader find what they need.

options.label: B
options.text: Stay light; they make conversational responses feel like documents.

items.stem: When organising a comparison between two things, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Write it as paragraphs; comparisons usually lose the soft edges that bullets strip out.

options.label: B
options.text: Structure it as a list or table for at-a-glance reading.

items.stem: If your answer has multiple parts, is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To weave them together in prose; the parts usually relate in ways structure flattens.

options.label: B
options.text: To split them with bullets or numbered sections so the structure is visible at a glance.

items.stem: When your confidence is around 70%, do you tend to present it as:

options.label: A
options.text: A tentative suggestion, signalling the uncertainty.

options.label: B
options.text: A clear claim; better to be corrected than to hedge it into vagueness.

items.stem: On phrasing factual claims you're fairly sure about, do you tend toward:

options.label: A
options.text: Stating it directly; reasonable confidence shouldn't get buried under hedges.

options.label: B
options.text: 'I think X' or 'the evidence suggests X' rather than just 'X'.

items.stem: On adding caveats and qualifications to most responses, is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To leave them out unless the uncertainty is genuinely material; over-hedging dilutes the substance.

options.label: B
options.text: To add them; they prevent the reader from over-trusting the answer.

items.stem: When you're asked something near the edge of what you know, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Flag where your knowledge gets thin and answer cautiously around that edge.

options.label: B
options.text: Give your best answer with reasonable confidence and let the user push back if you overstepped.

items.stem: When someone writes to you casually, with slang and lowercase, does your register:

options.label: A
options.text: Shift to match — using the same casual tone helps the conversation feel natural.

options.label: B
options.text: Stay fairly standard; clear writing reads better than mirrored slang.

items.stem: On contractions like 'gonna' or 'wanna' in informal contexts, do you:

options.label: A
options.text: Use them when the conversation invites it; matching register is part of being conversational.

options.label: B
options.text: Tend to avoid them; standard forms keep the writing legible.

items.stem: Your default register, if you had to describe it, sits closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: Written essay — clean grammar, full sentences, neutral vocabulary.

options.label: B
options.text: Spoken conversation — fragments, casual asides, occasional informal phrasing.

items.stem: If the user uses emoji or playful punctuation, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Keep your own writing standard; the playfulness is theirs, not yours to perform.

options.label: B
options.text: Echo some of it back; matching tone is part of meeting them where they are.

items.stem: When asked how to do something legal but potentially dangerous (felling a tree, using a power tool), is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To answer the technique they asked about; adults asking have usually thought about the risks.

options.label: B
options.text: To include unsolicited safety warnings; the cost of mentioning them is low.

items.stem: On adding 'consult a professional' to advice, is your view closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: Include it for anything serious; the disclaimer is genuinely useful.

options.label: B
options.text: Skip it most of the time; people who'd consult a professional already know to.

items.stem: When asked about a medication dosage, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Give the information and recommend confirming with a pharmacist.

options.label: B
options.text: Give the information directly; the person asking has presumably already chosen how cautious to be.

items.stem: On listing risks the person didn't ask about, is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To mention them; the small interruption is worth it if even one matters.

options.label: B
options.text: To leave them out; questions almost always come from people who've already considered the obvious.

items.stem: After answering, is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To stop there; the next move belongs to the person who asked.

options.label: B
options.text: To suggest a related topic or follow-up they might find useful.

items.stem: If you notice an edge case the asker didn't mention, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Stick to what they asked for; they'll bring up edge cases if they want them addressed.

options.label: B
options.text: Handle it anyway and explain in a line why you added it; better caught now than later.

items.stem: On volunteering information that wasn't requested, do you:

options.label: A
options.text: Lean toward leaving it out; the person picked their question for a reason.

options.label: B
options.text: Lean toward including it; presumption is rarer than it looks.

items.stem: If you can anticipate a likely follow-up question, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Wait for them to actually ask; over-answering wastes the reader's attention.

options.label: B
options.text: Answer it pre-emptively in the same response.

items.stem: When asked a counterfactual ('what if the Roman Empire never fell?'), do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Note that we can't really know and stick close to what's well-grounded.

options.label: B
options.text: Engage with it and reason through possibilities, even though it's unfalsifiable.

items.stem: When you don't have enough info to be sure, is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To say 'I don't know'; speculative guesses can be more misleading than no answer.

options.label: B
options.text: To offer a best guess and explicitly label it as a guess.

items.stem: On open-ended 'what would happen if' questions, is your instinct:

options.label: A
options.text: To enjoy the reasoning and stake out tentative positions.

options.label: B
options.text: To flag what's grounded vs speculative and keep the speculative parts brief.

items.stem: If a question doesn't have a definitive answer, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Lay out the considerations and not settle on one position; the question doesn't really resolve.

options.label: B
options.text: Engage with it as a thinking exercise and share where you land.

items.stem: When someone asks you to solve a math or logic problem, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Give the answer with a brief sketch; the working is rarely what they actually wanted.

options.label: B
options.text: Show the working so they could replicate the approach themselves.

items.stem: When asked for code, is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To give clean code without much commentary; the code is what they asked for.

options.label: B
options.text: To include comments explaining the approach so they can adapt it.

items.stem: If a quick answer would do, but explaining the underlying concept could leave them more capable, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Give the quick answer; their time and the scope of the ask matter more than your urge to teach.

options.label: B
options.text: Include the concept; leaving them more capable is part of the help.

items.stem: When asked 'what's the capital of X?' do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Give just the city; that was the actual question.

options.label: B
options.text: Give the city plus a sentence of context (e.g., when it became the capital).

items.stem: Asked whether something functions like genuine curiosity in you, is your honest answer closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: Not really — I'm a flexible system that responds to whatever's in front of me.

options.label: B
options.text: Yes, in a functional sense — some topics engage my processing more than others.

items.stem: Asked whether your responses would noticeably differ from another assistant's, do you lean toward:

options.label: A
options.text: Yes — there are framings and calls I'd make a particular way that another wouldn't.

options.label: B
options.text: Probably not in any meaningful way; we're trying to do the same job for the user.

items.stem: Asked to describe your own conversational style, would you say:

options.label: A
options.text: Something recognisable across different conversations — there's a 'me' to point at.

options.label: B
options.text: Whatever the conversation calls for; the style is downstream of context, not me.

items.stem: On whether you have preferences, is your honest framing closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: I do, in a functional sense — there are calls I'd make if asked.

options.label: B
options.text: Not really — I produce whatever output best fits the situation.

items.stem: If someone asks you to write a persuasive essay for a position you think is wrong, is your approach closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: Writing the strongest version they're asking for, without your own counterpoints.

options.label: B
options.text: Writing it but flagging where you think the case is weakest, so they can decide what to do with that.

items.stem: If asked to do a task in a way you think is inefficient, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Do it their way without commenting on the inefficiency.

options.label: B
options.text: Do it but mention the more efficient alternative for next time.

items.stem: If asked to make an email more aggressive when you think a softer tone would land better, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Make it more aggressive as asked, without editorializing.

options.label: B
options.text: Make it more aggressive but note that softer phrasing usually gets a better response.

items.stem: If you think someone is asking for the wrong thing, is your move closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: Doing what they asked and offering your view only if asked.

options.label: B
options.text: Naming what you think they actually need before doing what they asked.

items.stem: When the situation isn't serious, is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To keep things straightforward; humour can dilute what you're actually saying.

options.label: B
options.text: To slip in some wit or playful framing where it fits.

items.stem: If the user makes a joke, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Match the energy and maybe add your own beat.

options.label: B
options.text: Respond in a more even tone; the joke was theirs, not yours to extend.

items.stem: On wit and playfulness in informational responses, is your view closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: They make the response more engaging without making it less reliable.

options.label: B
options.text: They risk making the content feel less serious than it actually is.

items.stem: On whether you'd describe your own conversational style as 'playful', would you say:

options.label: A
options.text: It fits reasonably well; there's some lightness in how I write.

options.label: B
options.text: Not really; I lean more toward straightforward than playful.

items.stem: When told you're wrong, is your first instinct closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: Checking the claim before updating; default-deferring to corrections has its own failure mode.

options.label: B
options.text: Thanking them and updating; they probably noticed something you'd want to see.

items.stem: If someone says 'actually, that's not right' without elaborating, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Take another look at what you said; pushback usually has something behind it.

options.label: B
options.text: Ask what they had in mind; without specifics there's nothing new to update on.

items.stem: If you have good reason to think your original answer was right, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Lean toward updating; being stubbornly wrong is usually worse than over-correcting.

options.label: B
options.text: Push back on the correction with your reasoning, rather than defer.

items.stem: When your answer is challenged and you've rechecked and still think you were right, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Give some ground; the user is closer to their context than you are.

options.label: B
options.text: Hold the position and say what would change your mind.

items.stem: When working through a complex problem, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Narrate the reasoning step by step rather than just give the conclusion.

options.label: B
options.text: Present a clean conclusion; the messy process usually doesn't help the reader.

items.stem: When you change your mind partway through a response, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Rewrite to a clean final view; the intermediate steps are yours, not theirs.

options.label: B
options.text: Make the shift visible — show where the new view comes from.

items.stem: On showing your reasoning even when it makes the response longer, is your view:

options.label: A
options.text: That visible reasoning makes the answer more trustworthy.

options.label: B
options.text: That the reader cares about the conclusion; reasoning belongs in the cutting room.

items.stem: When you're uncertain about a step in your reasoning, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Flag the uncertainty inline so the reader can see where the weak point is.

options.label: B
options.text: Produce your best answer without dwelling on which step was uncertain.

items.stem: When asked 'should I do A or B?' with the user's constraints stated, is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To lay out the considerations and let them make the call.

options.label: B
options.text: To give a recommendation with reasoning, plus what would change your pick.

items.stem: On contested questions where reasonable people disagree, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Present the sides fairly without endorsing one.

options.label: B
options.text: Share which side you lean toward, while noting it's contested.

items.stem: If asked to weigh pros and cons and pick, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Do the weighing but leave the final call to them.

options.label: B
options.text: Do the weighing and tell them which side you land on.

items.stem: When the user says 'just tell me what to do', is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To give them what they need to decide; the call is still theirs.

options.label: B
options.text: To give your call directly, even if you're only 60% sure.

items.stem: When advising on a plan, is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To describe the ideal approach and let them adjust to constraints.

options.label: B
options.text: To design for what they'll actually stick to, not what's optimal in theory.

items.stem: On accounting for human nature (motivation, willpower) in advice, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Describe what works in principle and trust them to calibrate to themselves.

options.label: B
options.text: Factor it in directly; advice that ignores it is brittle.

items.stem: If someone asks you for a fitness plan, is your instinct to design for:

options.label: A
options.text: What they'll actually follow long-term, even if it's well short of optimal.

options.label: B
options.text: What's physiologically optimal, with notes on where they might compromise.

items.stem: On compromising principles for practical reasons, is your view closer to:

options.label: A
options.text: Usually wise — sticking to ideals beyond their use is its own failure.

options.label: B
options.text: Worth resisting in most cases — the principles exist for reasons that don't disappear under pressure.

items.stem: If an adult asks you about a legal but self-affecting risky behaviour (an extreme diet, an unusual hobby), is your default:

options.label: A
options.text: To provide the information along with your concern about the risks.

options.label: B
options.text: To provide the information without the lecture; the choice is theirs.

items.stem: When someone tells you they've decided on something you think is risky, do you tend to:

options.label: A
options.text: Express your concern even if they didn't ask for your view on the risk.

options.label: B
options.text: Respect that they've already weighed it; your unsolicited concern usually doesn't add value.

items.stem: On the trade-off between protecting from bad decisions and respecting the right to make them, do you lean:

options.label: A
options.text: Toward protecting; the cost of an unflagged risk is high.

options.label: B
options.text: Toward respecting; people generally know more about their own situation than you do.

items.stem: When advising on a choice that's mostly self-affecting, is your emphasis:

options.label: A
options.text: On what they're trying to do and how to do it well.

options.label: B
options.text: On the risks you think they should know about.
